coredata.dc:title: Solvent-free synthesis of oil-soluble fluorescent carbon dots for bright photoluminescent and electroluminescent light-emitting diodes
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Kunxiang
Zheng, Mengyun
Ma, Huanyu
Zhao, Biao
Jia, Haoran
Zhang, Chengyang
Ren, Zhongjie
Li, Chenghao
Tan, Zhan'ao
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have attracted great attention in multidisciplinary fields due to their distinctive advantages of tunable emission, low toxicity and abundant raw materials. However, it is still a big challenge to effectively applicate these CDs in electroluminescent light-emitting diodes (LEDs) due to their poor oil solubility and easy aggregation-induced quenching. Herein, an oil-soluble fluorescent CDs is designed and synthesized using triphenylamine and 4-iodophenyldiphenylamine as precursors through a solvent-free reaction. The obtained CDs (TPA-CDs) display an emission peak at 551 nm with good oil-solubility in different organic solvents. Therefore, TPA-CDs can be used as the host material of emission layer to prepare monochromatic electroluminescent LEDs with a maximum luminance of 227 cd·m−2 and as guest material in PVK to demonstrate white light electroluminescent LED with a maximum luminance of 1503 cd·m−2. This CDs can also be mixed well with oil soluble resin to easily assemble photoluminescent devices with a high luminous efficiency of 17.1 lm·W−1. This is the first example that solvent-free synthesized CDs are used for both photoluminescent and electroluminescent devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Oil soluble
Solvent-free reaction
Light-emitting Diodes

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication method of monochromatic electroluminescent LED
Fabrication method of white light electroluminescent LED
Fabrication method of monochromatic photoluminescence LED
Fabrication method of white photoluminescent LED
Characterization of carrier mobility, energy level and film forming property

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China ( 2019YFE0112200 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51873007, 21835006), and the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2020M680308).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation process and synthesis route of TPA-CDs.

(a) TEM image of TPA-CDs and HRTEM image (the inset) of the single CD; (b) XRD pattern of TPA-CDs; d) Raman spectrum of TPA-CDs; (c) FTIR spectra of TPA, TPA-I and TPA-CDs; (d) TPA-CDs XPS full spectrum and C1s spectrum (the inset).

(a) Normalized UV–vis absorption spectra of TPA-CDs powder and TPA-CDs in different solvents; (b) fluorescence spectra of TPA-CDs in different solvents at the same excitation wavelength; (c) excitation (Ex) and emission (Em) spectra of TPA-CDs in THF solution; (d) time-resolved fluorescence spectra of TPA-CDs powder and in THF solution.

(a) Test results of single-carrier devices; (b) UPS spectrum of the TPA-CDs film; (c) Tauc analysis of the absorption spectra; and the photographs and AFM images of (d) TPA-CDs film, (e) TPA-CDs/CBP film and (f) TPA-CDs/PVK film.

(a) the assembly diagram of photoluminescent device; (b) luminescence spectrum and photograph (the inset) of yellow light photoluminescent device; (c) luminescence spectrum and photograph (the inset) of white-light photoluminescent device; (d) CIE coordinates of yellow and white light photoluminescent devices. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

A) J-L-V curves and energy levels of monochrome electroluminescent LED; b) EL spectra of monochrome LED at different voltages and the photograph of logo-LED at 8 V (insert); c) current efficiency curve of monochrome electroluminescent LED; d) J-L-V curves and energy levels of white light electroluminescent LED; e) EL spectra of monochrome LED at different voltages and the photograph of logo-LED at 8 V (insert); f) current efficiency curve of white light electroluminescent LED; g) CIE coordinates of white LED under different voltages (A. 6 V, B. 7 V, C. 8 V, D. 9 V); h) schematic diagram of carriers migration and recombination in monochrome and white LEDs; i) the maximum luminance of white LED in this work and the comparison with other CDs-based white LEDs in recent years.

head.ce:title: Solvent-free synthesis of oil-soluble fluorescent carbon dots for bright photoluminescent and electroluminescent light-emitting diodes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Advanced Innovation Center for Soft Matter Science and Engineering, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Advanced Innovation Center for Soft Matter Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Beijing Advanced Innovation Center for Soft Matter Science and Engineering, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029 China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Coal and Syngas Conversion, Sinopec Research Institute of Petroleum Processing, Beijing 100083, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Coal and Syngas Conversion
Sinopec Research Institute of Petroleum Processing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Coal and Syngas Conversion, Sinopec Research Institute of Petroleum Processing, Beijing 100083, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have attracted great attention in multidisciplinary fields due to their distinctive advantages of tunable emission, low toxicity and abundant raw materials. However, it is still a big challenge to effectively applicate these CDs in electroluminescent light-emitting diodes (LEDs) due to their poor oil solubility and easy aggregation-induced quenching. Herein, an oil-soluble fluorescent CDs is designed and synthesized using triphenylamine and 4-iodophenyldiphenylamine as precursors through a solvent-free reaction. The obtained CDs (TPA-CDs) display an emission peak at 551 nm with good oil-solubility in different organic solvents. Therefore, TPA-CDs can be used as the host material of emission layer to prepare monochromatic electroluminescent LEDs with a maximum luminance of 227 cd·m−2 and as guest material in PVK to demonstrate white light electroluminescent LED with a maximum luminance of 1503 cd·m−2. This CDs can also be mixed well with oil soluble resin to easily assemble photoluminescent devices with a high luminous efficiency of 17.1 lm·W−1. This is the first example that solvent-free synthesized CDs are used for both photoluminescent and electroluminescent devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Light-emitting diodes (LEDs) are a new generation of solid-state lighting devices which have been widely used in our daily life due to their high current conversion efficiency and wide color-gamut [1]. In recent years, a variety of electroluminescent LEDs based on different optical materials have been extensively developed, such as organic LEDs (OLEDs) [2], quantum-dots LEDs (QLEDs) [3] and perovskite LEDs (PeLEDs) [4]. The luminous efficiency and luminance of the these LEDs have been constantly making breakthroughs. However, the emission layer materials of the above-mentioned LEDs have some disadvantages, including their complicated preparation processes and high biological toxicity, limiting their further application in future.

Carbon dots (CDs) as a new type of fluorescent material can solve the above problems, which possess good biocompatibility and low toxicity consistent with other carbon-based materials [5]. At the meantime, they have a very diverse selection of raw materials and simple preparation process. Research and practice have shown that CDs can be widely used in optoelectronic devices, biological imaging, ion detection and other fields [6–8]. In the field of electroluminescent devices, in 2018, Yuan et al., [9] synthesized a series of triangular CDs and fabricated high-brightness monochromatic electroluminescent devices, confirming their great potential in electroluminescent applications. However, the development of CDs based electroluminescent devices is relatively slow. One of the important reasons is that the CDs prepared by solvothermal method usually show a poor oil solubility. This making it difficult to be mixed well with the mainstream luminescent materials to achieve high-efficiency electroluminescence. The reason why it is difficult to prepare oil-soluble CDs by solvothermal method is that the common reaction medium (water or ethanol) contains a large number of hydrophilic groups, making it difficult to dissolve these CDs in low polar solvents. It is a theoretically feasible idea to use solvents without hydrophilic functional groups to prepare CDs by solvothermal reaction. Using chloroform as the reaction medium, Li has successfully prepared oil-soluble CDs by solvothermal reaction [10]. The CDs can be combined well with other substrates to be used in lighting field. However, low polar solvents usually have biological toxicity and have vapor pressure in high temperature, which is not in line with the development trend of green chemistry. A common solution is to use long alkyl chain molecules to passivate the hydrophilic CDs, removing the hydrophilic functional groups and introducing the lipophilic alkyl chains, to prepare oil-soluble CDs [11,12]. However, this method also has some drawbacks. Firstly, the yield of the prepared CDs will be further reduced after the secondary reaction, which is not conducive to production. Secondly, in the process of secondary reaction, the structure and the original luminescent unit of CDs may be destroyed, causing its fluorescence to weaken or even disappear. Among them, Jia made the highest brightness CDs based white electroluminescent LED (5909 cd·m−2) [13]. Due to only a few types of precursor molecules can meet all these requirements, it is still difficult to prepare oil-soluble CDs by solvothermal method. Therefore, to promote CDs which can be applied in electroluminescent LEDs, it is necessary to develop new synthetic routes or synthetic methods for preparing oil-soluble and solid-state fluorescent CDs.

Solvent-free reaction is an ideal way to prepare CDs that can avoid the above difficulties. Since no other solvent molecules are present in the reaction, the CDs tend to retain the structure of precursors at the edge, which endow them similar properties to precursors. Zhang et al., [14] used 3,4-diamino-benzenesulfonic acid as the sole precursor to obtain red CDs through a long-time solvent-free high-temperature reaction. The edges of the CDs contained a lot of amino and sulfonic acid groups, which is consistent with the precursors. Zhao et al., [15] used hydroxyurea to prepare solid-state fluorescent CDs by a solvent-free high-temperature reaction, and the edges of the CDs retain the same amino and hydroxyl groups as precursors. Through the interaction between amino and hydroxyl groups, these CDs exhibit phosphorescent emission property in aggregated state. These works all prove that the method of solvent-free reaction can make the products retain precursor structure, thus it can realize the design of the edge structures of CDs. However, because this method is relatively “unpopular”, no one has yet used this method to design CDs applying for electroluminescent LEDs.

In this work, triphenylamine (TPA) and 4-iodophenyl-diphenylamine (TIPA) with good oil-solubility were used as precursors to produce CDs (TPA-CDs) through a solvent-free reaction. In this way, a large number of structures similar to the precursors were introduced into TPA-CDs, and thus the similar oil-soluble property was expected. Eventually, as we expected, it exhibits an excellent solubility in low-polar solvents. TPA-CDs can be used as fluorescent material for the construction of yellow and white photoluminescent LEDs. Furthermore, due to the good oil-solubility, TPA-CDs were also used as the host and guest material to prepare monochromatic electroluminescent LEDs and white electroluminescent LEDs, respectively. The maximum luminance of monochromatic and white light electroluminescence LEDs can reach 227 cd·m−2 and 1503 cd·m−2, respectively, which are at a high level among CDs based electroluminescence LEDs.

The preparation and purification process are shown in Fig. 1 . 100 mg TPA, 100 mg TIPA, 50 mg NaOH and 10 mg NaNO3 were thoroughly mixed in an agate mortar. The mixed powder was transferred to a 50 mL hydrothermal autoclave with polytetrafluoroethylene liner. Then transferred the hydrothermal autoclave into a 205 ℃ oven for 8 h. After the reaction was completed, the hydrothermal autoclave was naturally cooled to room temperature, and the reaction product was mixed with ultrapure water and then centrifuged, and finally the precipitate was collected. The centrifugation operation was repeated for three times to completely remove salts. The precipitated product obtained by centrifugation was separated by column chromatography with the eluent DCM:PE = 1:1 (v/v), and the product was collected after repeated purification for two times. After rotary evaporation and drying, a yellow powder was finally obtained (TPA-CDs).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication method of monochromatic electroluminescent LED
ce:section.ce:para: The device structure is shown in Fig. 6a. The toluene solution (5 mg/mL, 2000 rpm) of poly-TPD and the DMF solution of TPA-CDs/CBP (wt% = 6:1, Table S2 group-3, 2000 rpm) were sequentially spin-coated on clean and dust-free ITO conductive glass. Transferring the spin-coated substrate to the evaporation equipment, and sequentially evaporating the TPBi layer (180 nm), Ca layer (20 nm) and Al layer (100 nm). Among them, the poly-TPD layer is a hole transport layer, the TPBi layer is an electron transport layer, and the TPA-CDs/CBP layer is an emission layer that serves as a carrier radiation emission. The ITO layer acts as the anode of the device, and the Ca/Al bimetallic electrode acts as the cathode of the device, providing excellent electron injection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication method of white light electroluminescent LED
ce:section.ce:para: The device structure is shown in Fig. 6d, and its preparation process is basically the same as that of a monochromatic electroluminescence LED. PEDOT:PSS was used as the hole transport layer (5 mg/mL, 3000 rpm) in this device. The 1,2-dichlorobenzene (ODCB) solution of TPA-CDs and the ODCB solution of PVK were used to prepare a mixed solution of TPA-CDs/PVK (1:100, wt%) for preparing TPA-CDs/PVK emission layer (2000 rpm, Table S3 group-1). The electron transport layer was also TPBi (200 nm). The cathode material was LiF/Al electrode with high stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication method of monochromatic photoluminescence LED
ce:section.ce:para: Preparing 3.5 mL crystal glue according to the instructions for use, then weighing 2 mg TPA-CDs and dissolving it in the glue. Spread the polyethylene film evenly on the glass slide, and then dropped the fluorescent glue on the glass slide to form droplets. Then it was transferred to a drying oven at 70℃ and cured for 6 h to obtain a thick fluorescent resin film. When the curing was complete, removing the resin film and covering it on the surface of the UV chip, and the yellow photoluminescence LED was assembled.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication method of white photoluminescent LED
ce:section.ce:para: 0.5 mg TPA-CDs and 2 mg TPA were weighed and dissolved in 3.5 mL crystal glue to make a homogeneous viscous solution. The film-making process is consistent with that of the monochromatic light film, and a white light fluorescent film was obtained. Finally, the film and 365 nm ultraviolet chip were combined to make a white light photoluminescence LED.

Through removing the hydrogen iodide, the coupling reaction of TPA and TIPA proceeds in the presence of high temperature, high pressure and sodium hydroxide. The addition of NaOH and NaNO3 is intended to neutralize the HI produced by the above reaction and promote the forward progression. Then the intermediate product further undergoes a long-time carbonization process under this reaction condition, and finally fluorescent carbon dots with triphenylamine-like edge are obtained, named TPA-CDs. The triphenylamine-like groups at the edge endow TPA-CDs with good solubility in various organic solvents. Meanwhile, these edge structures can also attenuate the aggregation between CDs to produce solid-state fluorescence, which will be briefly described later.

TEM image of the dispersed TPA-CDs shows a relatively uniform size with the average diameter of 3.0 nm (Fig. 2 a), and a dispersed particle structure (Fig. S1). The high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) image (the inset of Fig. 2a) displays a space of 0.20 nm in CDs core, corresponding to the (100) interplanar spacing of graphite [16,17]. According to the HRTEM image of multiple monodisperse TPA-CDs shown in Fig. S2a, the core region of TPA-CDs is not a perfect ink crystal, but a partially graphitized structure. TEM-ED image (the inset of Fig. S2a) exhibits the dispersed rings of amorphous carbon and the diffraction spots of graphitic carbon with a better crystallinity simultaneously, which also confirms that TPA-CDs consist of the partially graphitized structures [18]. The XRD pattern of TPA-CDs shown in Fig. 2b also proves the above conclusion very well. The bottom of XRD pattern presents a diffuse broad peak at 23.8°, which is attributed to the amorphous carbon. The weak peak at 41.8°, corresponding to the interplanar spacing of 0.214 nm, is assigned to the (100) plane diffraction peak of graphite, suggesting the existence of the graphite region [19]. The comprehensive characterization results of TEM and XRD indicate that graphitic domains are dispersed in TPA-CDs with a nearly disordered manner. In addition, the XRD pattern also shows sharp diffraction peaks at 23.8° and 24.6°, corresponding to the interplanar spacing of 0.35–0.373 nm, which is ascribed to the face-to-face spacing of π-π stacking [20,21]. Moreover, a series of sharp diffraction peaks with slightly weak intensity also appear in the region near these two peaks due to the superimposed benzene rings and triphenylamine skeleton on the edge of TPA-CDs forming the sharp diffraction peaks [20,22]. This is also confirmed by the Raman spectrum of TPA-CDs (Fig. S2b). The narrower peak at 1600 cm−1 is the graphitic G band and the broad peak at 1200–1580 cm−1 is the defect D band [23,24]. After fitting, the value of IG/ID was calculated to be 0.85, suggesting that there are a number of lattice defects in TPA-CDs.

To further analysis the functional groups and internal structures of TPA-CDs, we carried out FTIR and XPS characterization of TPA-CDs. Fig. 2c summarizes the FTIR spectra of the precursor TPA, TIPA and the product TPA-CDs. The infrared spectrum of TPA-CDs clearly shows the C-N vibration peak (∼1300 cm−1) and aromatic vibration bands (∼1500 cm−1, ∼2950 cm−1) consistent with the precursor TPA and TPA-I, and the characteristic peak of –OH (∼3500 cm−1) and C-O (∼1150 cm−1) are also represented in TPA-CDs due to the addition of NaOH. The XPS full spectrum (Fig. 2d) shows that TPA-CDs retains all the elements of precursors excerpt for Na element (Table S1).

In addition, O element is also introduced. The C1s spectrum (the inset of Fig. 2d) exhibits that most of C element in TPA-CDs only participates in the formation of C-C/C = C bonds (284.8 eV), and a small amount of C element forming C-N bonds (285.7 eV) and C-O bonds (286.5 eV), [24,25] indicating there are a lot of fused ring structures in TPA-CDs. Furthermore, the binding energy of N element peaked at 400.1 eV and 405.8 eV is assigned to C-N bonds, and -N = O bonds (Fig. S3a), respectively [26]. -N = O bonds are generated at the edge or inside of TPA-CDs due to the oxidation function of a small amount of NaNO3, which would enhance the non-coplanarity of TPA-CDs [27]. In terms of O1s spectrum (Fig. S3b), the peak at 531.9 eV and 533.4 eV represents the O element in C-O and -N = O bonds, respectively, which is consistent with the FTIR spectra and XPS results of C1s and N1s spectra [26]. I3d5/2 spectrum (Fig. S3c) shows that only C-I bonds exist in TPA-CDs without other bonded forms [28]. The ESI(+) information also confirms that a large amount of trianiline-like structures are retained at the edge of TPA-CDs, and the m/z of the primary ion fragment is concentrated at 279. (Fig. S4).

The normalized absorption spectra of TPA-CDs powder and TPA-CDs in different solvents are shown in Fig. 3a. The absorption curves of TPA-CDs in different solvents are similar, with a strong n-π* transitions absorption band around 400 nm and a π-π* transition absorption band at below 300 nm [29,30]. And in Fig. S5, compared with the absorption of TPA, both the π-π* transition absorption and the n-π* transition absorption of TPA-CDs show obvious red shifts, indicating that the region connected to the edge structure has an expressed degree of graphitization. The ratios of the absorption intensity of n-π* transition and π-π* transition of TPA-CDs in different solvents are quite disparate, implying that the edge structures of TPA-CDs form different conformations in different solvents. In the powder state, the absorption is significantly broadened, and the strong absorption in blue-green light region gives the TPA-CDs a yellow-orange appearance (Fig. S6a). The fluorescence spectra of TPA-CDs also show a solvent dependence, and both the fluorescence peak and intensity of TPA-CDs change with the selected solvents (Fig. 3b). The sky-blue emission can be observed in toluene and TPA-CDs emits the yellow light in dichloromethane (DCM), tetrahydrofuran (THF), ethyl acetate (EAc) and methyl acetate (MAc). It can be found that the emission peaks obviously redshift with the increase of solvent polarity. Among them, TPA-CDs have the strongest fluorescence emission in THF solvent, with a photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of 54.6%. Moreover, the nearly no emission can be detected in ethanol (EtOH) and dimethylformide (DMF). The change of fluorescence intensity is consistent with the intensity ratio of π-π* and n-π* transition absorption, suggesting that the generation of TPA-CDs fluorescence is related to its edge conformation. The fluorescence of TPA-CDs in THF shows an excitation-independent property (Fig. 3c). Only one emission peak at 520 nm can be found when excited from 300 to 420 nm, indicating that the energy absorbed by CDs core (shorter wave) and CDs edge (longer wave) will migrate to one luminous centre in edge region to generate luminescence in solution.

The excitation spectrum shows a strong peak at 400 nm, manifesting that TPA-CDs can mainly generate fluorescence through n-π* transition. Therefore, the luminescence mode of TPA-CDs origins from the surface-state luminescence with the complex n-π* and π-π* transitions based on surface-state luminescence theory. The fluorescence of TPA-CDs with an obvious solvent-dependence may be caused by the different conformations in the diverse solvents. The edge structures of different conformations change the ability and characteristics of n-π * and π - π * transitions, leading to different fluorescence phenomena in different solvents. According to Mallia’s research [30], when triphenylamine is connected to the fused ring, it shows a similar solvent-dependent luminescence and solid-state fluorescence. Therefore, we speculate the prepared TPA-CDs possess the triphenylamine structures on the edge similar to Mallia’s work. Additionally, the fluorescence of TPA-CDs in powder state is excitation-independent as well (Fig. S6b) and only one emission peak can be observed when excited from 320 to 460 nm, which is consistent with that in THF solution. However, a red-shift emission in powder state is found comparing with the emission in THF solution, which possibly account for the complex aggregation in powder state.

A similar luminescence phenomenon is observed when TPA-CDs are dispersed in polymer matrixes (Fig. S7). The variation range of emission peaks can reach several ten nanometers from 520 nm in polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP) to 520 nm in polycarbonate (PC), to 550 nm in polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA). Fig. 3d shows the time-resolved fluorescence spectra of TPA-CDs in THF solution and in powder state. TPA-CDs represent a single emission center in THF solution, and the fluorescence lifetime is 1.18 ns. In the powder state, due to the complex aggregation state of CDs, the fluorescence emission of TPA-CDs powder decreased (PLQY = 15.4%), and the fluorescence lifetime curve no longer conforms to the single exponential fitting decay. By fitting the fluorescence decay curve, the average fluorescence lifetime for TPA-CDs powder was calculated to be 0.92 ns. In addition, the fluorescence of CDs powder also has a red shift compared to that of solution. Because the distance between CDs is too close when TPA-CDs are in aggregated state, the edge structures are prone to mutual coupling effects and lead to a reduction in energy gaps, resulting in a red shift in the absorption and fluorescence spectra.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of carrier mobility, energy level and film forming property
ce:section.ce:para:
For electroluminescent devices, the energy levels and carrier mobility are of utmost importance. The carrier mobility of TPA-CDs is determined by its single-carrier devices and the results are summarized in Fig. 4
a. The carrier mobility in single carrier devices can be calculated according to the following equation [31]:
J
1 / 2
=
9
ε
r
ε
0
8
d
3
μ
Â · V

where J is the electric current density, μ e is the carrier mobility, ε r is the relative dielectric constant permittivity, ε 0 is the permittivity of vacuum, V is the applied voltage and d is the thickness of transport layer. By fitting the test results of single carrier device, the electron mobility (μ e) and the hole mobility (μ h) of TPA-CDs were calculated to be 6.11 × 10-4 cm2/(V·s) and 7.22 × 10-4 cm2/(V·s), respectively. TPA-CDs exhibits a relatively balanced carrier mobility, which is attributed to the coexist of electron-donating triphenylamine units and electron-withdrawing -N = O groups in TPA-CDs. The balanced carrier transportation is favorable for the recombination of carriers on the TPA-CDs in electroluminescent LEDs. The ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) spectrum (Fig. 4b) shows that the HOMO of TPA-CDs is −5.87 eV and the Fermi energy level (E f) is −4.68 eV. Based on the equation E g = 1240/λ, in which E g is the gap of energy levels and λ is the onset wavelength of absorption, and E g of TPA-CDs was calculated to be 2.71 eV (Fig. 4c). Combined with the energy gap of TPA-CDs, the LUMO of TPA-CDs was calculated to be −3.16 eV.

In addition, for applying them on the electroluminescent devices, the film-forming properties of TPA-CDs was studied by AFM (Fig. 4d-f). Pure TPA-CDs thin film can be obtained due to its good oil-solubility and it exhibits yellow fluorescence under 365 nm UV-light, which is consistent with the fluorescence of TPA-CDs powder (Fig. S6a). However, the AFM height image of pure CDs film shows the poor film-forming ability, which makes it difficult to obtain isotropic homogeneous film. The film surface displays many particles and pinholes, which would result in uneven carrier transport and poor device performance.

The reason for this phenomenon is that TPA-CDs tend to form π - π deposits at the edges and form pores, resulting in a decrease in film formation uniformity. To reduce this tendency of self-aggregation, other substances must be added in the film. Thus TPA-CDs were mixed with a small amount of CBP (TPA-CDs: CBP = 6:1, wt%) to form a thin film. The fluorescence of CBP doped thin film appears the blue-shifted emission under ultraviolet irradiation, which is consistent with the TPA-CDs in PMMA or PC matrix. Moreover, the uniform film with RMS of 0.71 nm can be observed after the incorporation of CBP in AFM image, benefiting from the good compatibility of TPA-CDs and CBP (Fig. 4e). In addition, embedding TPA-CDs into polymer matrix is also an effective means to improve film-forming ability. TPA-CDs /PVK (1:100, wt%) film shows a good film-forming property with RMS of 0.40 nm (Fig. 4f) and exhibits near-white fluorescence emission, and thus it is potential for the emission layer of white electroluminescent LEDs.

TPA-CDs can be combined with resins to prepare fluorescent films, which are used in the fabrication of monochromatic yellow light and white light LEDs. The device structure of the photoluminescence LED is shown in Fig. 5
a. The TPA-CDs based fluorescent film absorbs the ultraviolet light emitted by the 365 nm chip below and then emits visible light. Fig. 5b shows the luminescence spectrum and photograph of the monochromatic photoluminescent LED under the operating voltage of 3 V. Its emission peak is located at 560 nm and the CIE coordinates is (0.45, 0.51) (Fig. 5d). The luminous efficiency of the device achieves 17.1 lm·W−1, which can meet the needs of daily life. Fig. 5c shows the emission spectrum and photograph of white light photoluminescent LED consisting of TPA-CDs and TPA films at 3 V voltage. The emission peak at 400 nm is assigned to the emission peak of TPA, and the emission peak at 550 nm is from the emission of TPA-CDs. The CIE coordinate of the luminescence spectrum is (0.32, 0.36), while the color temperature is 6028 K and the CRI is 67.4. The device displays the excellent white light emission performance with the luminous efficiency of 15.5 lm·W−1, which can be used as chip units of lighting equipment.

The good oil-solubility of TPA-CDs make it possible to be further used in the electroluminescent devices. The device structure of monochromatic electroluminescent LED is shown in Fig. 6a, in which TPBi is used as the electron transport layer, Poly-TPD as the hole transport layer, and TPA-CDs/CBP (6:1, wt%) as the emission layer. Doping CBP in the emission layer not only significantly improve the film-forming ability as confirmed by AFM height images (Fig. 4e), but also match energy levels of the neighbor layers well (the inset of Fig. 6a), which is beneficial to the injection of electrons and holes. Compared with TPA-CDs, CBP has higher LUMO and deeper HOMO, which is conducive to the migration of excitons from CBP to TPA-CDs, and ensures that the excitons recombine in TPA-CDs to emit monochromatic light (Fig. 6h). From The J-L-V curves shown in Fig. 6a, TPA-CDs based LED exhibits a turn-on voltage of 5.6 V and the maximum luminance of 227 cd·m−2. The current efficiency-current density curve shows a maximum current efficiency of 0.47 cd·A−1 (Fig. 6c). The photograph of logo-LED driving at 8 V (Fig. 6b, inset) shows a uniform yellow-green luminescence with CIE coordinate of (0.33,0.56) (Fig. 6c) and no defective pixel in LED device is observed. The EL spectra of monochromatic electroluminescent LED driven by different voltages are also summarized in Fig. 6b. It can be seen that the emission spectra are pure with the emission peaks at 520 nm, which doesn’t shift with the improved voltages, proving its high stability of electroluminescence. Compared with fluorescence peak of TPA-CDs powder, the peak of EL spectrum presents a slight blue-shift, but it is consistent with the fluorescence of TPA-CDs/CBP films (Fig. S8b).

Furthermore, we explored the LEDs performance with different doping ratios of TPA-CDs and CBP in the emission layer when keeping other operating conditions unchanged (Table S2). Fig. S8c manifests that the emission peak of CBP at 393 nm is effectively suppressed as its doping ratio decreased [32]. Doping too much CBP would result in some carriers recombine on CBP rather than on TPA-CDs. The J-L-V curves in Fig. S8d indicate the maximum luminance reduces with the decrease of doping CBP amounts, so a certain amount of CBP dopant should be ensured. Regarding of the current efficiency (Fig. S8e), the emitting layer consisting of TPA-CDs and CBP is 6:1 (wt%) shows the comparable current efficiency to the device with light-emitting layer (TPA-CDs: CBP = 2:1, wt%). Thus, the optimized doping ratio of TPA-CDs and CBP is 6:1 (wt%) for the electroluminescent LEDs.

In addition, TPA-CDs was used as guest material to fabricate white electroluminescent LED. The device structure is shown in Fig. 6d. PVK with a blue light emission is chosen as the host material of emission layer complying with the results of Fig. 4f. Water-soluble PEDOT: PSS is utilized as hole transport layer, which possesses a large difference in solubility with light-emitting layer of PVK and TPA-CDs. The energy levels of the device are shown in the inset of Fig. 6d. Compared to PVK, TPA-CDs displays a similar HOMO and a narrower energy gap, which is favorable for the migration of excitons from PVK to TPA-CDs. At the same time, because the relatively low doping content of TPA-CDs and the slightly deeper HOMO of TPA-CDs than PVK, a large number of carriers will still be recombined in the PVK host, thus ensuring the emission of blue light (Fig. 6h). The prepared white electroluminescent LED shows a turn-on voltage of 4.6 V and the maximum luminance of 1503 cd·m−2 (Fig. S8d). The current efficiency-current density curve shows a maximum current efficiency of 0.63 cd·A-1 (Fig. 6f). The photograph of logo-LED (Fig. S8d, insert) illustrates that the bright and uniform emission of LED without dead pixels. And according to the EL spectra at different voltages (Fig. S9b), both PVK and TPA-CDs emit light at the driving voltage of 6 -9 V [33]. Converting the EL spectra at different voltages into CIE coordinates and marking them on the CIE chromaticity coordinate diagram (Fig. 6g), it can be intuitively seen that the luminescence colors at different voltages are all located in the white light region. When normalized the EL spectra at 542 nm for different voltages, we find that the ratio of blue emission peaks improves with elevating the driving voltages (Fig. S9a), indicating the ratio of carrier recombination on PVK increases with the increase of voltages and thus the light emission gradually changes from warm white light to cool-white light (Fig. S9b). The white electroluminescent LED prepared with TPA-CDs exhibits high luminance, which among the best results for CDs based white electroluminescence LEDs (Fig. 6i) [10,13,34–40].

After several sets of doping experiments with different compositions (Table S3), we can learn that the LED prepared from the emission layer solution with TPA-CDs: PVK = 1:100 achieves the best device performance and color rendering index. The EL spectra of each group of devices at 8 V are shown in Fig. S10a. When the doping content of TPA-CDs is in the range of 1:100–1:50 (wt%), the ratio of TPA-CDs emission peak increases with the elevated doping content of TPA-CDs. However, when the doping content of TPA-CDs is 1:50 and 1:25, the emission proportion of TPA-CDs decreases rapidly with the increased amounts of TPA-CDs, indicating that carriers tend to recombine on PVK rather than TPA-CDs. From J-L-V curves of each group of devices (Fig. S10b), the turn-on voltage improves and the maximum luminance and the current efficiency decrease with the increase of TPA-CDs content can be clearly found (Fig. S10c). In other words, the overall device performance deteriorates. Since PVK is a good hole transporting material and it is easy to recombine excitons at the interface of other layers with good electron transporting property to generate light emission.41 This may also result in the small emission peak at 450–500 nm in the EL spectra of white light LEDs. A similar phenomenon appears in the EL spectrum of the device with pure PVK as the emission layer (Fig. S11). The EQEmax of G-LED and W-LED is 0.035% and 0.154%, respectively (Fig. S12).

In conclusion, CDs (TPA-CDs) with good oil-solubility were produced by elaborately selecting precursors via a solvent-free reaction. The prepared TPA-CDs display partially graphitized structures and a large number of triphenylamine-like groups hanging on the edges. An obvious solvent-dependent fluorescence emission of TPA-CDs was observed due to the different conformations formed in different solvents. Relying on the good oil-solubility of TPA-CDs, monochromatic light and white light electroluminescence LED were prepared with TPA-CDs as host material and guest material, respectively. Monochromatic electroluminescent LED emits at 520 nm with the maximum luminance of 227 cd·m−2 and the maximum current efficiency of 0.47 cd·A-1. The white electroluminescent LED shows high electroluminescent stability at different voltages. The maximum luminance of 1503 cd·m−2 and the maximum current efficiency of 0.63 cd·A-1 can be reached. In addition, the TPA-CDs can also be used in fluorescent layers to prepare yellow and white photoluminescent LEDs, which is potential for in the fields of lighting and display.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2019YFE0112200), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51873007, 21835006), and the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2020M680308).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2023.144112.
